claim: Context poisoning, distraction, confusion, and clash are key failure modes when context grows; monitoring for these patterns is essential in agent design
actionable: When building agents, test for context failure modes: verify models don't over-focus on long context (distraction), don't conflate contradictory info (clash), and don't repeat hallucinations (poisoning).

claim: RAG (retrieve relevant documents) should use vector similarity to fetch semantically relevant chunks, not all available context, reducing irrelevance
actionable: Implement RAG with semantic chunking and vector search to retrieve only k most relevant documents (4-8); avoid dumping entire corpus into context.

claim: Context pruning (removing irrelevant info via LLM) and summarization (condensing via LLM) both risk information loss and must be validated carefully
actionable: When using pruning/summarization for context reduction, use small models for speed but validate retained info; Cognition uses fine-tuned models to prevent data loss.

claim: Context offloading (storing info in files, scratchpads, or external stores) preserves information outside LLM context window for later retrieval
actionable: Use files, state objects, or persistent stores (Postgres/Redis) to offload agent notes, plans, and research summaries; retrieve as needed to avoid context bloat.

claim: Tool loadout (semantic retrieval of relevant tools based on task) reduces context confusion from overlapping tool definitions in large tool sets
actionable: For agents with many tools, embed tool descriptions and retrieve only semantically relevant tools per query; don't bind all tools to every LLM call.

claim: Multi-agent quarantine (isolating subtasks into separate context windows) risks inconsistency across agents; best for decoupled tasks like research (not tightly coupled decisions)
actionable: Use multi-agent only for loosely coupled information gathering (research, search) not for tightly coupled decisions; mitigate conflicts by doing synthesis in single final agent.

claim: Token accumulation from repeated tool observations causes context bloat; context engineering approaches aim to reduce unnecessary info while preserving key facts
actionable: Monitor token growth in agent loops; apply pruning, summarization, or offloading strategically to keep context window <70% capacity for reasoning.
